guide.does-nail-polish-expire.title: Does Nail Polish Expire?
guide.does-nail-polish-expire.desc: Nail polish doesn't rot, but it thickens and separates over time. Here's how long it really lasts and how to tell when to toss it.
guide.does-nail-polish-expire.s0.b0: Nail polish has no water and few nutrients for bacteria, so it doesn't 'go bad' like makeup. Instead the solvents evaporate, the formula thickens, and the pigment separates.
guide.does-nail-polish-expire.s0.b1: Opened polish typically stays usable for 1–2 years; unopened, well-stored bottles can last much longer.
guide.does-nail-polish-expire.s1.b0: - It's stringy, gloopy or won't level out on the nail.
guide.does-nail-polish-expire.s1.b1: - The colour and pigment have separated and won't remix by rolling the bottle.
guide.does-nail-polish-expire.s1.b2: - It smells strongly of chemicals or dries oddly.
guide.does-nail-polish-expire.s1.b3: Thinner can revive a slightly thick polish, but not a fully separated one — never use acetone for this.
guide.does-nail-polish-expire.s2.b0: Keep bottles upright, tightly capped, and away from heat and sunlight. Wipe the neck so the cap seals — a loose cap is what dries polish out early.
guide.does-nail-polish-expire.s3.h: What is actually happening in the bottle
guide.does-nail-polish-expire.s3.b0: Nail polish is a suspension: pigment and a film-forming resin (traditionally nitrocellulose) carried in volatile solvents. It works by having the solvent evaporate off the nail, leaving the film behind. Everything that goes wrong with a bottle over time is a version of that happening too early.
guide.does-nail-polish-expire.s3.b1: Solvent escapes past an imperfectly sealed cap, and the remaining polish thickens. Pigment, being heavier, settles out — which is normal and reversible. The resin itself can slowly stiffen, which is not.
guide.does-nail-polish-expire.s3.b2: Because there is essentially no water in the formula, microbial spoilage is not a factor. This is the one product in the cabinet where "expired" is a performance judgement rather than a hygiene one, and the only thing at risk is your manicure.
guide.does-nail-polish-expire.s4.h: Reviving a thick polish — and when not to
guide.does-nail-polish-expire.s4.b0: Roll the bottle between your palms rather than shaking it. Shaking whips air bubbles into the formula, and those bubbles end up as craters in the finish. Rolling redistributes settled pigment without aerating it.
guide.does-nail-polish-expire.s4.b1: If it is genuinely too thick, a few drops of proper nail polish thinner will restore it. Thinner replaces the evaporated solvent.
guide.does-nail-polish-expire.s4.b2: Never use nail polish remover for this. Acetone is a solvent, but it is the wrong one — it dissolves the resin film rather than carrying it, and a bottle cut with acetone will apply streaky and chip within a day. It is the single most common piece of bad advice in this area.
guide.does-nail-polish-expire.s4.b3: A polish that has separated into a clear layer and a dense sludge that will not recombine after several minutes of rolling is finished. So is one that has gone stringy or gummy — that is the resin, and no solvent brings it back.
guide.does-nail-polish-expire.s5.h: Why the cap is the whole game
guide.does-nail-polish-expire.s5.b0: Almost every polish that dies early dies of a bad seal. Polish creeps into the threads of the neck as you use it, dries there, and holds the cap fractionally open — and then the solvents leave over the following months.
guide.does-nail-polish-expire.s5.b1: Wipe the neck and the threads with a lint-free pad and a little remover before you cap it. Thirty seconds, and it is the difference between a two-year bottle and a six-month one. Store upright, cool, and out of the sun, which also keeps the pigment from fading.
guide.does-nail-polish-expire.faq0.a: Not really — the worst case is a lumpy, streaky manicure. It's a quality issue, not a safety one.
guide.does-nail-polish-expire.faq1.q: Can I use nail polish remover to thin my polish?
guide.does-nail-polish-expire.faq1.a: No. Acetone breaks down the resin film rather than replacing the evaporated solvent, so the polish applies streaky and chips almost immediately. Use a purpose-made nail polish thinner — a few drops is enough.
guide.does-nail-polish-expire.faq2.q: Why is my new polish full of bubbles?
guide.does-nail-polish-expire.faq2.a: Shaking. Roll the bottle between your palms instead — shaking beats air into a formula that will trap it as it dries.
guide.does-nail-polish-expire.also0: Coty batch codes (Sally Hansen, Rimmel, Max Factor)
guide.does-nail-polish-expire.also2: How to dispose of expired makeup
guide.how-to-dispose-of-expired-makeup.t: How to Dispose of Expired Makeup
guide.how-to-dispose-of-expired-makeup.d: Old cosmetics shouldn't just go in the bin. Here's how to empty, sort and recycle expired makeup and skincare responsibly.
guide.how-to-dispose-of-expired-makeup.s: Empty before you toss
Scrape or wash out leftover product first — most recyclers won't take containers with residue. Liquids can go down the drain in small amounts; solids go in general waste.
Never pour large amounts of oil-based product down the sink, as it can clog pipes.
Sort the packaging
- Glass bottles and jars: rinse and recycle with glass.
- Plastic tubes and pumps: check the resin code; pumps often aren't recyclable and go to general waste.
- Compacts and mirrors: usually mixed materials — separate the metal pan if you can.
- Aerosols (dry shampoo, setting spray): empty fully, then recycle with metals where accepted.
Take-back and recycling programs
Several beauty retailers and brands run in-store take-back schemes for hard-to-recycle cosmetic packaging. Dropping empties there is the cleanest option.
Going forward, decoding batch codes when you buy helps you use products up before they expire — less waste to dispose of in the first place.
Why cosmetic packaging defeats normal recycling
A shampoo bottle is one material and recycles easily. Almost nothing else in a make-up bag is one material.

A pump is a plastic body, a metal spring, a smaller plastic dip tube and often a gasket — four materials fused into a component nobody can separate at speed on a sorting line, so the whole thing goes to landfill. A compact is plastic, a glued metal pan and a glass mirror. A mascara is a tube, a wiper, a wand and a cap in different plastics. A lipstick bullet is a barrel, a swivel mechanism and a spring.

This is why so much cosmetic packaging is technically recyclable and practically is not: recycling depends on being able to sort a material, and mixed-material items cannot be sorted. Where you can pull the components apart — unscrew a pump, prise the metal pan out of a compact — you convert an unrecyclable object into two or three recyclable ones. Where you cannot, general waste is the honest destination and pretending otherwise contaminates the recycling stream.
What to do with the product itself
- Water-based liquids (toner, micellar water, light lotions): small quantities down the drain with plenty of water.
- Oils, balms and thick creams: do not pour these down the sink. Scrape into general waste — oil congeals in pipes and is a genuine cause of blockages.
- Powders: tip into general waste. Rinse the pan afterwards.

- Aerosols: never puncture or crush them, and never put a part-full can in with metals. Empty by discharging in a ventilated space, then recycle where cans are accepted, or take to a household hazardous waste point if not.
- Acetone, nail polish and remover: these are classed as hazardous waste in most jurisdictions and should go to a civic amenity site rather than a kerbside bin.
- Anything containing an active drug — prescription retinoids, some acne products — should go back to a pharmacy where a take-back scheme exists.
The best disposal is not needing to dispose

The majority of cosmetics that get thrown away were never finished. They were bought, used a few times, and drifted past their date in a drawer — and the environmental cost of that is far larger than any decision about which bin the empty goes in.

The habits that fix it are unglamorous: buy the size you will realistically use, check a batch code before buying discounted stock so you know how much life it has left, and keep the products with the shortest clocks — mascara, vitamin C, sunscreen — visible rather than buried.
guide.how-to-dispose-of-expired-makeup.f: Can I put a half-full aerosol in the recycling?
No. A pressurised can is a hazard on a sorting line and in a bin lorry. Discharge it fully in a ventilated space first, or take it to a household hazardous waste point.
Do brand take-back schemes actually recycle the packaging?

The good ones do, and they exist precisely because kerbside recycling cannot handle mixed-material beauty packaging. They are the right destination for pumps, compacts, mascaras and anything you cannot pull apart.
guide.brands-that-print-the-date.title: Brands That Print the Date Directly (Korean & French Pharmacy)
guide.brands-that-print-the-date.desc: Many Korean, Japanese and French-pharmacy brands stamp the manufacture or expiry date on the pack in plain numbers — here's how to read it without a batch-code decoder.
guide.brands-that-print-the-date.s0.h: Not every brand hides the date in a code
guide.brands-that-print-the-date.s0.b0: Western conglomerates (Estée Lauder, L'Oréal, Coty, LVMH and others) stamp a coded production date that needs decoding. But a large share of the market does the opposite: they print the manufacture or expiry date on the packaging as a plain calendar date.
guide.brands-that-print-the-date.s0.b1: This is especially common with Korean and Japanese skincare and with French-pharmacy brands. For these products you don't need a batch-code decoder at all — the date is right there once you know what to look for.
guide.brands-that-print-the-date.s1.h: How to read the printed date
guide.brands-that-print-the-date.s1.b0: Look on the base of the bottle or jar, the crimp at the end of a tube, or the flap of the box. You'll usually see two short dates or labels.
guide.brands-that-print-the-date.s1.b1: - Manufacture date: marked MFG, MFD, MAN, MD, M, or 제조 (Korean), 生産日期 (Chinese), 製造 (Japanese).
guide.brands-that-print-the-date.s1.b2: - Expiry / best-before: marked EXP, E, BB, or 까지 (Korean), 保质期 / 消费期限.
guide.brands-that-print-the-date.s1.b3: Common formats are YYYY.MM.DD, YYYY-MM-DD or DD.MM.YYYY. If only one date is shown, Korean and Japanese products usually print the manufacture date, while EU products more often print the expiry (period-of-minimum-durability) date.
guide.brands-that-print-the-date.s2.h: Which brands print the date directly
guide.brands-that-print-the-date.s2.b0: Korean skincare and makeup — for example Laneige, Innisfree, COSRX, Beauty of Joseon, Anua, Some By Mi, Torriden, Round Lab, Missha and most K-beauty labels.
guide.brands-that-print-the-date.s2.b1: Japanese brands such as Hada Labo, FANCL and DHC, and French-pharmacy lines such as Avène, Bioderma, Klorane, Ducray, A-Derma and Weleda, also print a readable date (some, like Bioderma, additionally encode it in the code).
guide.brands-that-print-the-date.s2.b2: Because these dates are printed in plain text, we don't run them through a decoder — reading the pack is faster and more reliable than any tool.
guide.brands-that-print-the-date.s3.h: Two dates, and which one to believe
guide.brands-that-print-the-date.s3.b0: Products that print dates often print two, and people routinely read the wrong one. The rule is simple: a manufacture date tells you how fresh the product was; an expiry date is the manufacturer's own guarantee. Where both exist, the expiry date wins, because it already accounts for the shelf life the manufacturer tested.
guide.brands-that-print-the-date.s3.b1: Where only one exists, its identity depends on where the product came from. Korean and Japanese packs usually print the manufacture date. EU packs, when they print a date at all, usually print a period-of-minimum-durability — a best-before. A bare date with no label is more likely to be an expiry in Europe and a manufacture date in Asia.
guide.brands-that-print-the-date.s3.b2: And a date beats a decode every time. Our checker exists to recover a manufacture date that a brand chose not to print. When the brand did print one, there is nothing to recover.
guide.brands-that-print-the-date.s4.h: Why the difference exists at all
guide.brands-that-print-the-date.s4.b0: This is a regulatory divide, not a cultural one. Korea's MFDS requires cosmetics to carry a manufacture date, so Korean manufacturers print it — on the pack, in the batch code, usually both.
guide.brands-that-print-the-date.s4.b1: EU regulation asks a different question. It requires a durability indication, and gives two ways to satisfy it: a best-before date if the product lasts 30 months or less, or the open-jar PAO symbol if it lasts longer. Since most cosmetics comfortably exceed 30 months sealed, most European products show a PAO and no date whatsoever — and the manufacture date survives only inside the batch code, which is where the decoding problem comes from in the first place.
guide.brands-that-print-the-date.s4.b2: French-pharmacy brands are the interesting middle case. They sit under the same EU rules as everyone else, but their products are sold in a pharmacy context where dates are expected, so many print one voluntarily. Bioderma both prints a date and encodes it in the code — belt and braces.
guide.brands-that-print-the-date.faq0.q: My Korean skincare has no batch code I can decode — is that normal?
guide.brands-that-print-the-date.faq0.a: Yes. Most Korean and Japanese brands print the manufacture date (제造) and often the expiry date directly on the pack instead of hiding it in a coded batch number, so there's nothing to decode — just read the printed date.
guide.brands-that-print-the-date.faq1.q: If both a manufacture and an expiry date are printed, which do I trust?
guide.brands-that-print-the-date.faq1.a: The expiry (or best-before) date is the manufacturer's own guarantee, so prefer it. The manufacture date tells you how fresh the product was and, with the open-jar PAO symbol, how long you have after opening.
guide.brands-that-print-the-date.faq2.q: What does the Korean word 제조 mean on my product?
guide.brands-that-print-the-date.faq2.a: 제조 means 'manufactured' — the date next to it is the production date. 까지 means 'until', marking the expiry date.
guide.brands-that-print-the-date.faq3.q: Why do Korean brands print the date when Western ones don't?
guide.brands-that-print-the-date.faq3.a: Regulation. Korea's MFDS requires a manufacture date on cosmetics, so brands print it. Most Western markets require only a durability indication, which a manufacturer can satisfy with the open-jar PAO symbol — so they do, and the date stays hidden in the batch code.
guide.brands-that-print-the-date.also2: Rohto batch codes (Hada Labo, Melano CC)
guide.where-is-the-batch-code-on-perfume: Where Is the Batch Code on Perfume?
The batch code on a perfume is a short stamp on the box and the bottle — here's exactly where to look on the packaging and how to read it.
On the box
The quickest place to find a perfume batch code is the outer box. Look on the bottom flap or one of the side panels, usually printed in small type near the ingredients list or the "made in" line.
It is a short, separate code of 4–8 letters and numbers — not the long barcode. It is often ink-jet printed, so it can look faint or slightly smudged.
On the bottle
If you no longer have the box, check the bottle itself. The code is usually laser-etched or printed on the base of the bottle, or on the clear sticker underneath it.
On tester and travel bottles the code can be on the crimp around the spray collar. Turn the bottle in good light — etched codes catch the light rather than showing as ink.
How to read it
Most fragrance houses encode the production date. Coty, Dior and many others use a year digit plus the day of the year (e.g. 4135 = the 135th day of 2024).
You don't need to decode it by hand — pick your brand on the home page, type the code, and the checker returns the manufacture date, age and estimated expiry.
Match the box code to the bottle code
When you have both, compare them. On genuine stock the two agree, because the box and the bottle were coded minutes apart at the end of the same filling line.

A mismatch means the box and the contents were brought together somewhere else. That happens with counterfeits, and it also happens with entirely legitimate repackaged and grey-market goods — a returned item reboxed, a tester rehoused, a bottle sold on without its original carton. Either way, it tells you that the pairing you are holding is not the one the factory shipped.
It is the single most useful check available to a buyer, it takes ten seconds, and it needs no tool.
Whose rule applies to your bottle
Fragrance is the category where the name on the bottle tells you least about who made it. Fashion houses license their perfumes, and the licensee's factory sets the format.
- Coty makes Hugo Boss, Calvin Klein, Gucci, Burberry, Chloé, Davidoff, Lacoste, Marc Jacobs, adidas and around thirty others. A flat four-digit code.
- Inter Parfums makes Montblanc, Jimmy Choo, Coach, Van Cleef & Arpels, Boucheron, Karl Lagerfeld, Kate Spade and Ferragamo. A nine-character code whose first letter is the year.
- L'Oréal makes Giorgio Armani, Prada, Valentino, Mugler, Azzaro, Viktor & Rolf, Ralph Lauren, Cacharel and Diesel fragrance. A year letter and a month character.
- LVMH's own houses — Dior, Guerlain, Givenchy, Kenzo, Loewe, Acqua di Parma — print the date rather than encoding it.
- Creed puts the year in a single letter and nothing else.
Select the brand in the checker and it applies the right rule automatically. If you want to understand what it is doing, each format has a page of its own.
Is the perfume batch code the same as the barcode?
No. The barcode is the long striped EAN number used at checkout and is identical on every bottle. The batch code is a separate short stamp that changes with each production run and encodes the date.
My perfume has no visible code — why?
Some codes are etched faintly on the glass base or hidden under the peel-off sticker. If there's genuinely no code, the bottle may be a tester, a refill, or a counterfeit.
The code is etched into the glass and I can't read it.

Laser etching has no ink — it only appears when light rakes across it from the side. Don't shine a light straight at it; tilt the bottle against a lamp or window until the characters catch. A phone camera with flash and zoom often captures what the eye can't.

guide.where-is-the-batch-code-on-tubes-a: Where Is the Batch Code on a Tube or Cream?
On tubes the batch code sits on the crimp at the sealed end; on jars and creams it's stamped on the base or lid. Here's where to look and how to read it.
On a tube

For creams, cleansers and sunscreens in a tube, look at the crimp — the flat sealed end opposite the cap. The batch code is embossed or printed there, often together with the expiry or a factory mark.
It's a short code of letters and numbers. On soft tubes it can be pressed into the metal or plastic, so tilt it under light to read it.
On a jar or pot
For creams in a jar, the code is usually stamped on the base of the jar or on the underside of the lid — not on the paper label.
It sits near the open-jar PAO symbol (e.g. 12M), which tells you how many months the product lasts once opened.
How to read it
Skincare houses each stamp dates differently — L'Oréal, Beiersdorf (Nivea, Eucerin) and Korean brands all use their own format.
Choose the brand on the home page and enter the code; the checker applies that brand's rule and shows the manufacture date and estimated shelf life.
Reading a code pressed into metal or plastic
Tube crimps are the hardest surface in the cabinet to read, because the code is usually pressed rather than printed — there is no ink, only a deformation of the material.

The instinct is to shine more light at it. That is exactly wrong: light from the front flattens the impression and makes it disappear. What you want is light from the side, so the ridges cast small shadows. Tilt the crimp under a lamp or towards a window and rotate it slowly; the characters will appear at one particular angle and vanish at every other.

For an aluminium crimp, a phone camera with flash held slightly off-axis works well. Photograph it, then zoom into the still image. Failing that, a soft pencil rubbed lightly across a piece of thin paper laid over the crimp will take a rubbing, the same way it does with a coin.
The three codes on a skincare tube
A crimp often carries more than one marking, and only one of them is the batch code.
- The batch code: irregular, alphanumeric, added at fill time.
- A best-before or expiry date: printed as a plain date. Where this exists, it supersedes any decoded estimate — it is the manufacturer's own guarantee.

- A mould or tooling number: a fixed number identifying the tube itself, not the batch. It never changes between production runs, which is how you can tell it apart if you have two of the same product.

French-pharmacy skincare — La Roche-Posay, Vichy, Avène, Bioderma — very often prints a genuine expiry date alongside the code. Use it, and treat the batch code as a way of knowing how fresh the product was when you bought it.
What is the crimp on a tube?
The crimp is the flat, sealed end of a tube opposite the cap. Manufacturers stamp the batch code and often the expiry date into this seam.
The code on my tube rubbed off — what now?
Ink-jet codes on tubes can wear away. Check the box if you still have it; otherwise the open-jar PAO symbol is your best guide to remaining shelf life.
There are two numbers on my crimp. Which is the batch code?

The irregular alphanumeric one. A number that is purely numeric and identical across two units of the same product is a mould or tooling number, not a batch code — it identifies the tube, not the production run.

guide.where-is-the-batch-code-on-lipstic: Where Is the Batch Code on Lipstick & Makeup?
On lipstick the batch code is on the base of the case; on compacts and palettes it's inside or on the back. Here's where to find it and how to read it.
On lipstick

Twist the lipstick closed and look at the flat bottom of the case — the batch code is printed or stamped there, sometimes alongside the shade number and, on Korean products, a plain 제조 (manufacture) date.
It's a short code separate from the shade name. Good light helps: on glossy cases the print can be low-contrast.
On compacts, palettes and mascara
For powders, blushes and palettes, check the back of the compact or the inside of the lid. For mascara and liquid liners, the code is on the bottom of the tube or where the label wraps.
As with all makeup, the code sits near or under the open-jar PAO symbol.
How to read it
Makeup makers such as Estée Lauder (MAC, Bobbi Brown), Coty (Rimmel, Max Factor, Bourjois) and L'Oréal each use their own date format.
Enter the code with the brand selected on the home page and the checker returns the manufacture date, age and how much shelf life is left.
The code hides in a different place on each format
Makeup is the most varied packaging category there is, and the code moves accordingly:
- Lipstick bullet: the flat base of the case, usually stamped into the plastic. Twist the bullet fully down first so the case sits flat.
- Liquid lipstick and gloss: around the base of the tube, or on the shoulder where the label ends.
- Pressed powder, blush, bronzer: the back of the compact. On some brands it is inside, under the pan — you can see it only when the powder is used up.
- Palettes: the back of the case, or on the cardboard sleeve if it shipped with one.
- Mascara and liquid liner: the base of the tube, or printed vertically where the label wraps.
- Cushion compacts: on the underside of the outer case, and often a second code inside on the refill itself.
The rule that holds across all of them: the code is near the open-jar PAO symbol, because both are added at the same stage of production.
Why a MAC code looks nothing like a Rimmel code
Two lipsticks, both bought in the same shop, can carry codes that look like they come from different planets — because they do. The format belongs to whoever manufactured the product.
A MAC, Clinique or Bobbi Brown code is three characters, like A56: a plant letter, a month and a year digit. Read it from the right, because the plant is the part that varies in length.
A Rimmel, Bourjois, Max Factor, CoverGirl or Sally Hansen code is four digits, like 4135: a year digit followed by the day of the year. All of them are Coty.
A Maybelline, NYX, Urban Decay, Essie or 3CE code is six characters with a letter in the middle, like 22U401: the letter is the year and the character after it is the month. All of them are L'Oréal.
Learn those three and you can read most of a make-up bag by eye.
Where is the batch code on a MAC or Estée Lauder lipstick?
On the base of the bullet case — a short plant-month-year code (e.g. A56). Enter it with the brand selected to decode the manufacture date.
Does makeup show an expiry date?
Rarely. Most makeup only carries a batch code plus the open-jar PAO symbol, so the code is how you find out when it was made.
My compact has no code on the outside.
Check inside the lid, and under the pan. Some brands print it on the metal pan itself, which only becomes visible once the powder is low — annoying, but it is there.

dec.coty-batch-code-format.title: Coty Batch Codes: Find & Check Yours
dec.coty-batch-code-format.desc: Find the batch code on Rimmel, Bourjois, Hugo Boss, Gucci, Calvin Klein and 35+ other Coty brands, and check the production date and estimated shelf life in seconds — free, no manual decoding.
dec.coty-batch-code-format.anat0: Last digit of the production year — 2024
dec.coty-batch-code-format.anat1: Day of that year (001–366) — day 135 is 14 May
dec.coty-batch-code-format.ex0: The textbook Coty code: day 135 of a year ending in 4.
dec.coty-batch-code-format.ex1: Day 001 — 1 January, the lowest day number a valid code can carry.
dec.coty-batch-code-format.ex2: Day 366 exists only in a leap year: 2024 had one, so this is 31 December.
dec.coty-batch-code-format.s0.b0: Coty uses one of the shortest formats in the industry: four digits, nothing else. The first digit is the last digit of the production year. The remaining three are the Julian day — the day's position in that year, counted from 001 on 1 January to 365 (or 366 in a leap year) on 31 December.
dec.coty-batch-code-format.s0.b1: That is the entire cipher. There is no plant code, no month letter and no line number to interpret. Coty applies it across everything it manufactures, which is why the same rule reads a Rimmel mascara, a Hugo Boss eau de toilette and an adidas body spray.
dec.coty-batch-code-format.s0.b2: Because the day is encoded exactly, a Coty code gives you the manufacture date to the day — better precision than most of the industry, where month-level accuracy is the norm.
dec.coty-batch-code-format.s1.b0: - Take the first digit and find the most recent year that ends in it. A code starting with 4 read in 2026 means 2024, not 2014 — unless the product is obviously older.
dec.coty-batch-code-format.s1.b1: - Take the last three digits as a day number. Divide by roughly 30.4 to get the approximate month: day 135 ÷ 30.4 ≈ 4.4, so early in the 5th month, May.
dec.coty-batch-code-format.s1.b2: - Sanity-check the result against the product. A code that decodes to a date before the product line launched is a red flag worth taking seriously.
dec.coty-batch-code-format.s2.b0: On fragrance, look at the base of the outer box and at the clear sticker on the underside of the bottle — the code is usually printed in small black or grey ink on both, and the two should match. A mismatch between box and bottle is a classic sign of repackaged or grey-market stock.
dec.coty-batch-code-format.s2.b1: On colour cosmetics (Rimmel, Bourjois, Sally Hansen, Max Factor, CoverGirl) the code is stamped or embossed on the back of the pack, sometimes on the crimp of a tube. Embossed codes are unlit and low-contrast: photograph them with light raking across the surface rather than head-on.
dec.coty-batch-code-format.s2.b2: The code is never the long barcode number under the black stripes. That is the EAN, which identifies the product for sale and carries no date.
dec.coty-batch-code-format.s3.h: What the format cannot tell you
dec.coty-batch-code-format.s3.b0: The year is a single digit, so it repeats every ten years. A code reading 4135 is 14 May 2024 — but it is also, on identical evidence, 14 May 2014. The decoder assumes the most recent plausible year; on a bottle that looks a decade old, subtract ten years.
dec.coty-batch-code-format.s3.b1: The code records when the product was made, not how long it stays usable once you open it. That is the PAO symbol — the open-jar icon marked 12M, 24M and so on. A three-year-old sealed bottle can be fine; a mascara opened eight months ago is not, whatever its batch code says.
dec.coty-batch-code-format.faq0.q: My Coty code has letters in it. Is it still readable?
dec.coty-batch-code-format.faq0.a: Yes. Some plants add letters around the four digits for internal tracking. The decoder isolates the 4-digit group and ignores the rest — the letters carry no date.
dec.coty-batch-code-format.faq1.q: Why does my code decode to a date in the future?
dec.coty-batch-code-format.faq1.a: It doesn't — the decoder rejects future dates and steps the year back a decade instead. If you are reading it by hand and land in the future, the year digit belongs to the previous decade.
dec.coty-batch-code-format.faq2.q: Do all Coty brands use this format?
dec.coty-batch-code-format.faq2.a: Every Coty-manufactured line we have verified does. Licensed fragrances made under contract elsewhere occasionally arrive with a different stamp; if the four digits decode to an absurd day (over 366), the code is not a Coty YDDD code.
dec.loreal-batch-code-format.title: L'Oréal Batch Codes: Find & Check Yours
dec.loreal-batch-code-format.desc: Check the batch code on Maybelline, Lancôme, Kiehl's, CeraVe, La Roche-Posay, YSL and 30+ more L'Oréal brands — get the production date and estimated shelf life in seconds, free.
dec.loreal-batch-code-format.anat0: Factory / plant identifier — no date information
dec.loreal-batch-code-format.anat1: Production year, from the annual letter cycle — U = 2021
dec.loreal-batch-code-format.anat3: Production batch series — no bearing on shelf life
dec.loreal-batch-code-format.ex0: Factory 22, year letter U, month 4 — April 2021.
dec.loreal-batch-code-format.ex1: Year letter X — two years on from U.
dec.loreal-batch-code-format.ex2: Month O is October, not a zero.
dec.loreal-batch-code-format.s0.b0: L'Oréal group codes are typically six characters, and unlike Coty's pure-digit stamp they mix numbers and letters. The leading digits identify the factory. The first letter is the year. The character immediately after that letter is the month. Whatever follows is an internal batch series with no date meaning.
dec.loreal-batch-code-format.s0.b1: The year runs on an annual letter cycle through the alphabet with the letter V skipped, so U = 2021, W = 2022, X = 2023, Y = 2024, Z = 2025, and the cycle wraps back to A for 2026. Skipping V is a deliberate choice: it is too easily confused with U on a low-contrast stamp.
dec.loreal-batch-code-format.s0.b2: The month character reuses digits 1–9 for January to September, then switches to letters for the last quarter: O = October, N = November, D = December. Those are the initials of the month names in French and English alike, which is a handy way to remember them — and a trap if you read the O as a zero.
dec.loreal-batch-code-format.s1.b0: - Find the first letter in the code. That is the year. Count forward from U = 2021 through the alphabet, remembering to skip V.
dec.loreal-batch-code-format.s1.b1: - Look at the very next character. A digit from 1 to 9 is the month directly. An O, N or D is October, November or December.
dec.loreal-batch-code-format.s1.b2: - Ignore everything before the letter and everything after the month character. The factory number and the batch series tell you nothing about age.
dec.loreal-batch-code-format.s2.b0: On tubes and bottles, the code is usually inkjet-printed on the crimp seal at the bottom of the tube, or on the bottle's base. On cartons it sits on one of the end flaps, often next to a printed best-before or PAO symbol.
dec.loreal-batch-code-format.s2.b1: L'Oréal's dermatological brands — La Roche-Posay, Vichy, CeraVe, SkinCeuticals — frequently print an explicit expiry date as well as the batch code. When a real expiry date is on the pack, trust it over any decoded estimate.
dec.loreal-batch-code-format.s2.b2: The stamp is often pale grey on white plastic. Tilt the pack against a light source rather than adding more light straight on.
dec.loreal-batch-code-format.s3.h: What the format cannot tell you
dec.loreal-batch-code-format.s3.b0: The code gives the month, not the day, so the decoder estimates mid-month. That is accurate enough for shelf-life purposes: no cosmetic's usability turns on a fortnight.
dec.loreal-batch-code-format.s3.b1: The letter cycle is 25 years long, so ambiguity is far less of a problem here than with single-digit year codes — but a very old product from a previous cycle would read as a recent one. In practice, a L'Oréal product old enough to hit that ambiguity is well past using.
dec.loreal-batch-code-format.s3.b2: The factory digits are sometimes read online as a date. They are not. Two products made the same week in different plants carry different leading digits.
dec.loreal-batch-code-format.faq0.q: My L'Oréal code has no letter in it at all.
dec.loreal-batch-code-format.faq0.a: Then it is not this format. Some L'Oréal-owned brands acquired recently (and some regional contract manufacturers) still print a plain Julian date. Our decoder falls back to reading an embedded date when the year-letter pattern isn't found.
dec.loreal-batch-code-format.faq1.q: Is the first letter always the year, even if the code starts with a letter?
dec.loreal-batch-code-format.faq1.a: Yes — the year is the first letter that appears anywhere in the code, whether or not factory digits precede it.
dec.loreal-batch-code-format.faq2.q: Does this work for Maybelline and NYX too?
dec.loreal-batch-code-format.faq2.a: Yes. Maybelline, NYX, Garnier, Essie, 3CE, Kiehl's, Lancôme, YSL Beauty, Urban Decay and the rest of the group share L'Oréal's plants and this code.
dec.estee-lauder-batch-code-format.title: Estée Lauder Batch Codes: Find & Check Yours
dec.estee-lauder-batch-code-format.desc: Check the batch code on Estée Lauder, Clinique, MAC, La Mer, Jo Malone, Tom Ford and Bobbi Brown — get the production date and estimated shelf life in seconds, free.
dec.estee-lauder-batch-code-format.anat2: Last digit of the production year
dec.estee-lauder-batch-code-format.ex0: Plant A, month 5 (May), year ending in 6.
dec.estee-lauder-batch-code-format.ex2: Month letter A means October, not the plant.
dec.estee-lauder-batch-code-format.s0.b0: Estée Lauder group codes are three characters — occasionally four, when the plant identifier takes two letters. Read them from the right: the last character is the year digit, the one before it is the month, and everything to the left is the plant.
dec.estee-lauder-batch-code-format.s0.b1: The month follows the industry's most common shorthand: digits 1 to 9 for January through September, then A, B and C for October, November and December, because there are no single digits left.
dec.estee-lauder-batch-code-format.s0.b2: This is why reading the code from the left gets people into trouble. In the code EA4, the A is not a plant — it is October. The plant is E, the month is A, and the year ends in 4.
dec.estee-lauder-batch-code-format.s1.b0: - Ignore everything except the last two characters.
dec.estee-lauder-batch-code-format.s1.b1: - Second-to-last character: 1–9 is that month number; A, B or C is October, November or December.
dec.estee-lauder-batch-code-format.s1.b2: - Last character: the year's final digit. Pick the most recent year that ends in it.
dec.estee-lauder-batch-code-format.s2.b0: On skincare jars and bottles, the code is stamped on the base — often embossed rather than printed, so it catches light rather than ink. On MAC and Bobbi Brown colour cosmetics it is on the back of the compact or the base of the lipstick barrel.
dec.estee-lauder-batch-code-format.s2.b1: Jo Malone and Tom Ford fragrance carry it on the box base and on the bottle's underside sticker, the same convention the rest of the fragrance industry uses.
dec.estee-lauder-batch-code-format.s2.b2: Three characters is short enough that people mistake other stamps for the batch code. If what you are reading is longer than four characters, it is probably a factory line marking, not the date code.
dec.estee-lauder-batch-code-format.s3.h: What the format cannot tell you
dec.estee-lauder-batch-code-format.s3.b0: Month precision only — the decoder estimates the 15th of the month. And a single-digit year repeats every decade, so a code decoding to May 2026 could in principle be May 2016. The decoder assumes the recent reading; the product's condition and packaging design usually settle which is right.
dec.estee-lauder-batch-code-format.s3.b1: The plant letter is genuinely uninformative for shelf life. It matters to Estée Lauder's quality control and to nobody else.
Does this work for La Mer?
Yes — La Mer, Clinique, Origins, Aveda, Smashbox, Too Faced, Dr.Jart+, Le Labo and the rest of the group all use the group's plants and this code.
My code is 4 characters. Which two matter?
The last two, always. A longer plant identifier just adds characters on the left.
Is a Clinique code the same as an Estée Lauder one?
The format is identical. The plant letter may differ because a given product is made on a given line, but the month/year rule is the same.
dec.dior-lvmh-batch-code-format.title: Dior, Chanel & LVMH Batch Codes: Find & Check Yours
dec.dior-lvmh-batch-code-format.desc: Check the batch code on Dior, Chanel, Guerlain and other LVMH beauty houses — enter it and get the production date and estimated shelf life in seconds, free.
dec.dior-lvmh-batch-code-format.anat0: Last digit of the production year — 2025 (or 2015; the digit repeats each decade)
dec.dior-lvmh-batch-code-format.anat1: Month letter — A = January … M = December, skipping I, so H = August
dec.dior-lvmh-batch-code-format.anat2: Day-or-batch number within that month — not certain, so only the month is read
dec.dior-lvmh-batch-code-format.ex0: Modern Dior: year digit 5, month letter H (August).
dec.dior-lvmh-batch-code-format.ex1: Modern Dior: year digit 9 (2019), month letter K (October).
dec.dior-lvmh-batch-code-format.ex2: A sister house / vintage form: two year digits, then day-of-year.
dec.dior-lvmh-batch-code-format.s0.h: Dior's own code: year digit, month letter
dec.dior-lvmh-batch-code-format.s0.b0: Since 1998, Christian Dior fragrance and cosmetics have used a compact code that starts with the last digit of the production year, then a letter for the month. The month letters run A = January, B = February, on through M = December, skipping I to avoid confusion with the digit 1. So 5H03 is an August 2015-or-2025 batch and 9K44 is October 2019.
dec.dior-lvmh-batch-code-format.s0.b1: The two digits after the month letter are a day-or-batch number — Dior is not consistent about which — so the reliable read is the year and month, not a precise day. And because the year is a single digit, it repeats every ten years; the decade is inferred from the bottle itself.
dec.dior-lvmh-batch-code-format.s1.h: Siblings and Chanel: the date in the digits
dec.dior-lvmh-batch-code-format.s1.b0: The other LVMH beauty houses — Guerlain, Givenchy, Kenzo, Loewe, Maison Francis Kurkdjian — and Chanel, which publishes no scheme at all, tend to print a production date straight into an all-digit code instead. That can be two year digits followed by the day of the year (24045), or a packed calendar date in YYMMDD form (231122 = 22 November 2023). The decoder reads those directly.
dec.dior-lvmh-batch-code-format.s1.b1: Acqua di Parma uses its own five-character code: three digits for the day of the year, then one digit for the year, then a plant letter. So 2480Y is the 248th day of 2020 (4 September 2020) and 0525S is the 52nd day of 2025 (21 February 2025).
dec.dior-lvmh-batch-code-format.s1.b2: Reading one by hand: for Dior, take the first digit as the year and the letter as the month (A=1 … M=12, no I). For the all-digit houses, a run of day-of-year digits divided by about 30.4 gives the month. If two readings both look valid, prefer the one that produces a date the product could plausibly have.
dec.dior-lvmh-batch-code-format.s2.b0: Fragrance: the base of the outer box, and a small sticker or laser etch on the underside of the bottle. On Chanel, the code is often laser-etched into the glass base itself and needs a raking light to read.
dec.dior-lvmh-batch-code-format.s2.b1: Makeup and skincare: the base of the compact, the crimp of the tube, or the underside of the jar.
dec.dior-lvmh-batch-code-format.s2.b2: The box code and the bottle code should agree. On authentic stock they nearly always do; a mismatch is one of the few genuinely reliable counterfeit signals available to a buyer.
dec.dior-lvmh-batch-code-format.s3.h: What the format cannot tell you
dec.dior-lvmh-batch-code-format.s3.b0: A 4-digit code carries a single year digit and therefore repeats every ten years. The decoder assumes the recent decade.
dec.dior-lvmh-batch-code-format.s3.b1: Because the houses publish nothing, we are reading a pattern, not a documented standard. Older stock and some regional codes do not follow it and will not decode — that is a limitation of the format, not a sign the product is fake.
dec.dior-lvmh-batch-code-format.s3.b2: The date is the manufacture date. Fragrance is more forgiving than skincare: kept away from light and heat, an unopened bottle is typically good for around five years, and many improve for the first year in the bottle.
dec.dior-lvmh-batch-code-format.faq0.q: Does Chanel really not have a batch code system?
dec.dior-lvmh-batch-code-format.faq0.a: Chanel has never published one. What is verifiable is that current Chanel products carry a readable production date in the code, and that is what we decode — labelled as a medium-confidence read, because there is no official scheme to check it against.
dec.dior-lvmh-batch-code-format.faq1.q: My Dior code is a digit, a letter, then numbers (like 5H03).
dec.dior-lvmh-batch-code-format.faq1.a: That is the standard modern Dior code: the first digit is the year, the letter is the month (A = January to M = December, skipping I), and the rest is a day-or-batch number. We read the year and month from it. Codes with an unusual letter (R, S, U…) come from some vintage or special-import runs where the month letter shifts, and may not decode.
dec.dior-lvmh-batch-code-format.faq2.a: Dior, Guerlain, Givenchy Beauty, Kenzo Parfums, Loewe Perfumes, Acqua di Parma, Maison Francis Kurkdjian, Maison Margiela, Fresh, Benefit — plus Chanel, and other houses that print the date rather than encode it.
dec.puig-batch-code-format.title: Paco Rabanne, Jean Paul Gaultier and Zara Batch Codes (Puig)
dec.puig-batch-code-format.desc: Check the batch code on Paco Rabanne, Jean Paul Gaultier, Carolina Herrera, Nina Ricci and Zara fragrance (Puig) — get the production date and estimated shelf life free.
dec.puig-batch-code-format.anat0: Last digit of the production year — 2024
dec.puig-batch-code-format.anat1: Day of that year — day 135 is 14 May
dec.puig-batch-code-format.ex0: The common 4-digit form: year digit, then day of year.
dec.puig-batch-code-format.ex1: A 5-digit variant: two year digits, then the day.
dec.puig-batch-code-format.s0.h: One manufacturer, five very different labels
dec.puig-batch-code-format.s0.b0: Paco Rabanne (now styled Rabanne), Jean Paul Gaultier, Carolina Herrera, Nina Ricci and Zara's fragrance line have no obvious connection — a Spanish fashion house, a French couturier, a Venezuelan-American designer, a Parisian maison and a high-street retailer. They share a manufacturer: Puig, the Barcelona family group that owns or licenses all of them.
dec.puig-batch-code-format.s0.b1: That is why an Invictus and a Zara eau de toilette carry the same style of code. As everywhere in fragrance, the format follows the factory rather than the logo.
dec.puig-batch-code-format.s1.b0: Puig does not run a proprietary cipher. It prints the production date inside the batch code, most often as a year digit followed by the day of the year — 4135 is the 135th day of a year ending in 4, that is 14 May 2024.
dec.puig-batch-code-format.s1.b1: Some lines print a longer form instead: five digits (two year digits and the day) or a six-digit packed calendar date in YYMMDD order. Our reader tries each shape and reports which interpretation it used.
dec.puig-batch-code-format.s2.b0: - Four digits: the first is the year's last digit, the remaining three are the day of the year. Divide the day by about 30.4 for the month.
dec.puig-batch-code-format.s2.b1: - Five digits: the first two are the year, the last three the day.
dec.puig-batch-code-format.s2.b2: - Six digits: read as year, month, day.
dec.puig-batch-code-format.s2.b3: - If two readings both produce a valid date, prefer the one that makes sense for the bottle — a date before the fragrance launched is telling you the reading is wrong.
dec.puig-batch-code-format.s3.b0: On the base of the outer box, and on the underside of the bottle — printed on the base sticker or, on the heavier Paco Rabanne bottles, stamped into the glass or the metal. Zara's fragrance boxes carry it on the bottom flap.
dec.puig-batch-code-format.s3.b1: The box code and the bottle code should agree. They are applied minutes apart on the same line, and a mismatch is a sign the box and the bottle were paired somewhere other than the factory.
dec.puig-batch-code-format.s4.h: What the code cannot tell you
dec.puig-batch-code-format.s4.b0: The four-digit form carries only one digit of year, so it repeats every decade. The decoder assumes the most recent reading; a bottle that clearly belongs to an older era may be ten years older than the decode suggests.
dec.puig-batch-code-format.s4.b1: And the code is a date, not a certificate. Paco Rabanne and Jean Paul Gaultier are among the most counterfeited fragrances in Europe, and a counterfeiter who copies a genuine code produces a code that decodes perfectly. Use the date as one signal, alongside the weight of the glass, the quality of the atomiser spray and the small print on the box.
dec.puig-batch-code-format.faq0.q: Is Zara perfume really made by Puig?
dec.puig-batch-code-format.faq0.a: Zara's fragrances are produced under a manufacturing arrangement with Puig, which is why they carry a Puig-style production code — and why several of them smell conspicuously like the designer fragrances Puig also makes.
dec.puig-batch-code-format.faq1.q: My Paco Rabanne code is on the box but not the bottle.
dec.puig-batch-code-format.faq1.a: On some formats it is stamped into the metal of the bottle rather than printed, and it can be genuinely hard to see. Tilt the bottle against a light so the stamp catches from the side rather than shining a light straight at it.
dec.puig-batch-code-format.faq2.a: The same house — the brand dropped 'Paco' from its name in 2023. Older stock carries the old branding, and the code format did not change.
dec.inter-parfums-batch-code-format.titl: Inter Parfums Batch Codes: Find & Check Yours
dec.inter-parfums-batch-code-format.desc: Check the batch code on Montblanc, Jimmy Choo, Coach, Van Cleef & Arpels, Boucheron and Karl Lagerfeld fragrances — get the production date and estimated shelf life free.
dec.inter-parfums-batch-code-format.anat: Production year, from a letter cycle that skips I and O — J = 2019
Day of the year — day 169 is 18 June
dec.inter-parfums-batch-code-format.s0.h: Why so many unrelated brands share a code
dec.inter-parfums-batch-code-format.s0.b: Montblanc, Jimmy Choo, Coach, Van Cleef & Arpels, Boucheron, Karl Lagerfeld, Kate Spade, Ferragamo, Moncler, Guess, Abercrombie & Fitch and Dunhill have nothing in common as fashion houses. They have everything in common as fragrances: Inter Parfums holds the licence and manufactures all of them.
That is the single most useful fact about batch codes generally. The format follows the factory, not the logo on the bottle. If you know who actually makes a fragrance, you know how to read its code.
dec.inter-parfums-batch-code-format.s1.b: The code runs to about nine characters. The first letter in it is the production year, on an annual cycle that skips I and O — the two letters most easily misread as 1 and 0. That puts J at 2019, K at 2020, L at 2021, M at 2022, N at 2023, P at 2024, Q at 2025 and R at 2026.
The last three digits are the day of that year. Everything in between is internal batch and line reference, and can be ignored.
dec.inter-parfums-batch-code-format.s2.b: - Find the first letter. Count from J = 2019 forward, skipping I and O.
- Take the final three digits as the day of the year and convert: divide by 30.4 for a rough month.
- If the final three digits are over 366, this is not the day — on a minority of Inter Parfums codes those digits are a factory line. Treat the result as approximate in that case.
dec.inter-parfums-batch-code-format.s3.b: Box base, and on a sticker or direct print on the underside of the bottle. Inter Parfums codes are usually printed in black on the white base sticker and are among the easier fragrance codes to read without a magnifier.
dec.inter-parfums-batch-code-format.faq0: Because a stamped I is indistinguishable from a 1 and an O from a 0 on a low-contrast print. Several manufacturers — Creed among them — skip the same two letters for the same reason.
dec.inter-parfums-batch-code-format.faq1: The last three digits give a date that seems wrong.
On some codes those digits are a line number, not a day. If the decoded date is implausible for the bottle, that is likely what happened. The year letter is the more reliable half of the code.
dec.creed-batch-code-format.title: Creed Batch Codes: Find & Check Yours
dec.creed-batch-code-format.desc: Check the batch code on any Creed fragrance — enter it and get the production year and estimated shelf life in seconds, free. See where the code is printed on the bottle and box.
dec.creed-batch-code-format.anat0: Product code — identifies the fragrance line (A42 is a classic men's line), not a date
dec.creed-batch-code-format.anat1: Production year — the two digits after the product code, so 21 = 2021
dec.creed-batch-code-format.anat2: Batch series within that year — no date information
dec.creed-batch-code-format.ex2: New F-series — F means 2023 or later; the exact year is not encoded.
dec.creed-batch-code-format.s0.b0: Creed has run two different systems, and neither encodes the month — a Creed code gives you a year at best.
dec.creed-batch-code-format.s0.b1: The classic code (roughly 2013–2022) is the longer one: a three-character product code, then the two-digit production year, then a batch series. In A4221N01, A42 is the product line, 21 is 2021, and N01 is the batch. In L6622A01B, the 22 is 2022.
dec.creed-batch-code-format.s0.b2: From 2023, Creed switched to a much simpler code that begins with F followed by a sequence number — F001704, F003235. The F marks the newer generation; the digits are a production-line sequence, not a date. So an F code tells you the fragrance was made in 2023 or later, but not exactly which year.
dec.creed-batch-code-format.s1.b0: - If the code starts with a letter then two digits (A42…, L66…), it is a classic code: read the two digits that come straight after the product code — that pair is the year (21 = 2021).
dec.creed-batch-code-format.s1.b1: - If the code starts with F followed by digits, it is the new series: you know it is 2023 or later, and higher sequence numbers (F003… over F001…) are later batches.
dec.creed-batch-code-format.s1.b2: - Either way, there is no month to extract.
dec.creed-batch-code-format.s2.h: Why the year alone is usually enough
dec.creed-batch-code-format.s2.b0: Fragrance is the one cosmetic category where a year of imprecision barely matters. A sealed Creed, stored away from sunlight and radiators, is typically fine for years — and the house's own reputation rests on juice that matures rather than spoils.
dec.creed-batch-code-format.s2.b1: Where the year does matter is provenance. Creed is among the most counterfeited fragrance houses in the world, and a code whose shape fits neither system is a straightforward tell.
dec.creed-batch-code-format.faq0.q: My Creed code is just an F and some numbers.
dec.creed-batch-code-format.faq0.a: That is the current F-series, used from 2023 onward. It confirms the bottle is from the newer generation but does not spell out the year, so the most anyone can say from an F code alone is 2023 or later.
dec.creed-batch-code-format.faq1.q: Can I get the month from the rest of the code?
dec.creed-batch-code-format.faq1.a: No. Neither Creed system encodes the month — the classic code gives a year, the F-series gives only 2023-or-later. Anyone claiming to derive a month is guessing.
dec.procter-gamble-batch-code-format.tit: Procter & Gamble Batch Codes: Find & Check Yours
dec.procter-gamble-batch-code-format.des: Check the batch code on Olay, Pantene, Head & Shoulders, Old Spice and Herbal Essences — get the production date and estimated shelf life in seconds, free.
dec.procter-gamble-batch-code-format.ana: Day of that year — day 32 is 1 February
dec.procter-gamble-batch-code-format.ex0: The 7-digit form: full year, then day. No ambiguity at all.
dec.procter-gamble-batch-code-format.ex1: The 4-digit form: one year digit, then day 099.
dec.procter-gamble-batch-code-format.ex2: The 5-digit form: two year digits, then day 045.
A Julian date is just a day number: 1 January is day 001, 31 December is day 365 (366 in a leap year). Procter & Gamble prints that day number together with the year, and the only thing that changes between products is how much of the year they print.

The seven-digit form spells the year out in full — 2026032 is the 32nd day of 2026, 1 February — and is the one to hope for, because it cannot be misread. The five-digit form uses two year digits. The four-digit form uses one, and inherits the decade ambiguity that comes with it.
The decoder tries all three and prefers the longest, most specific reading it can find, because more digits of year means less guessing.
- Split the code into year digits and a 3-digit day. The day is always the last three.
- Convert the day: divide by about 30.4 and round up for the month. Day 099 ÷ 30.4 ≈ 3.3, so early in the 4th month, April.
- If the year is a single digit, assume the most recent year ending in it unless the bottle looks a decade old.
Usually inkjet-printed on the bottle's shoulder, on the back label, or on the crimp of a tube — grey or black ink on plastic, often at an angle. On aerosols such as Old Spice and Secret it is stamped into the base of the can.
P&G products are mass-market and turn over quickly, so a code that decodes to more than two or three years ago on a shop shelf is unusual and worth a second look at the retailer.
dec.procter-gamble-batch-code-format.faq: Olay, Pantene, Head & Shoulders, Herbal Essences, Old Spice, Secret, Aussie, Native and the rest of P&G's beauty and grooming lines.
Is the Julian day the same as the date on a food product?
It's the same idea, and the same format many food and pharmaceutical manufacturers use. Once you can read a Julian date you can read a great many industrial codes.
dec.kenvue-batch-code-format.title: Neutrogena, Aveeno & RoC Batch Codes: Find & Check Yours
dec.kenvue-batch-code-format.desc: Check the batch code on Neutrogena, Aveeno and RoC (Kenvue) — enter it and get the production date and estimated shelf life free.
dec.kenvue-batch-code-format.anat0: Day of the year — day 123 is 2 May
dec.kenvue-batch-code-format.ex1: The short form: day 365, year digit 4 — the last day of the year.
dec.kenvue-batch-code-format.s0.h: The same digits, the opposite order
dec.kenvue-batch-code-format.s0.b0: Coty writes the year first and the day second. Kenvue does the reverse: the day of the year comes first, then the year. The digits look almost identical, which is exactly why reading a Neutrogena code with Coty's rule produces a confident, wrong answer.
dec.kenvue-batch-code-format.s0.b1: This is the strongest argument for knowing who manufactures a product before decoding its code. 12324 is day 123 of 2024 under Kenvue's rule and an impossible day 232 under Coty's. Sometimes the wrong rule produces a plausible-looking date rather than an obviously broken one — and then you have no way of knowing you got it wrong.
dec.kenvue-batch-code-format.s1.b0: - Take the first three digits as the day of the year (001–366).
dec.kenvue-batch-code-format.s1.b1: - Take what remains as the year: two digits is a full year (24 = 2024), one digit is the last digit of the year.
dec.kenvue-batch-code-format.s1.b2: - Convert the day to a month by dividing by roughly 30.4.
dec.kenvue-batch-code-format.s2.b0: On the crimp of the tube, the base of the bottle, or the back of the carton — usually inkjet, black on white. Neutrogena sunscreens also carry an explicit expiry date, which is required for sunscreens in most markets and should always take precedence over a decoded estimate.
dec.kenvue-batch-code-format.faq0.a: Yes, and unlike most cosmetics it is legally required to say so. The UV filters degrade; an expired sunscreen protects less than the label claims. Use the printed expiry date, not the batch code.
dec.kenvue-batch-code-format.faq1.a: Same products. J&J spun off its consumer-health division as Kenvue in 2023; the codes did not change.
dec.kenvue-batch-code-format.faq2.q: Is RoC still part of the same company?
dec.kenvue-batch-code-format.faq2.a: No — RoC was divested by Johnson & Johnson in 2019 and is now independent. It kept the J&J-era code format, which is why it still reads with this rule, but it is no longer a corporate sibling of Neutrogena and Aveeno. If a RoC code stops decoding cleanly on newer stock, that is the likely reason.
dec.unilever-batch-code-format.title: Unilever Batch Codes: Dove, Axe, Rexona, Vaseline
dec.unilever-batch-code-format.desc: Check the batch code on Dove, Axe, Rexona, Vaseline and other Unilever personal-care lines — get the production date and estimated shelf life in seconds, free.
dec.unilever-batch-code-format.anat0: Last digit of the production year — 2026
dec.unilever-batch-code-format.anat1: Day of that year — day 120 is 30 April
dec.unilever-batch-code-format.anat2: Plant / production line — no date information
dec.unilever-batch-code-format.ex0: Day 120 of a year ending in 6, made on line X.
dec.unilever-batch-code-format.ex1: The same format without a plant letter.
dec.unilever-batch-code-format.s0.b0: Unilever's mass personal-care products — Dove, Axe, Rexona, Vaseline, Sunsilk, TRESemmé, Simple, Pond's, St. Ives — use the same year-digit-plus-Julian-day pattern as Coty, with an optional trailing letter for the plant or line.
dec.unilever-batch-code-format.s0.b1: The first digit is the last digit of the year. The next three are the day of that year. A trailing letter, where present, is the production line and carries no date.
dec.unilever-batch-code-format.s1.b0: - First digit: the year's last digit. Pick the most recent year ending in it.
dec.unilever-batch-code-format.s1.b1: - Next three digits: the day of the year. Divide by about 30.4 for the month.
dec.unilever-batch-code-format.s2.b0: A minority of Unilever skincare plants use a month-letter-first variant rather than this format. Our decoder does not read those — it would have to guess, and guessing a date is worse than saying nothing.
dec.unilever-batch-code-format.s2.b1: If a Unilever code starts with a letter rather than a digit, you are looking at that variant. Check the pack for a printed best-before date instead.
dec.unilever-batch-code-format.faq0.q: Does deodorant really have a shelf life?
dec.unilever-batch-code-format.faq0.a: An unopened antiperspirant is typically good for around three years; the active salts and fragrance degrade slowly. It is one of the more forgiving categories.
dec.unilever-batch-code-format.faq1.q: My Dove bottle has two codes.
dec.unilever-batch-code-format.faq1.a: Common on bottles that are moulded in one plant and filled in another. The batch code is the inkjet-printed one, not the moulded number in the plastic.
dec.korean-batch-code-format.title: Korean Beauty Batch Codes: Find & Check Yours
dec.korean-batch-code-format.desc: Check the code on COSRX, Laneige, Innisfree, Beauty of Joseon and 40+ more K-beauty brands — most print the manufacture date, and our checker reads it for you.
dec.korean-batch-code-format.ex0: The 8-digit form: full year, month, day.
dec.korean-batch-code-format.ex1: The 6-digit form with a line letter: 22 November 2023.
dec.korean-batch-code-format.s0.h: Why Korean codes are the easiest to read
dec.korean-batch-code-format.s0.b0: Korea's regulator requires the manufacture date (제조) on cosmetic packaging. Manufacturers comply by printing it, and their batch codes generally lead with the same date rather than encoding it — as YYYYMMDD, YYMMDD, or occasionally YYMM followed by a line letter.
dec.korean-batch-code-format.s0.b1: So a K-beauty code is usually not a code at all. It is a date, in the order the rest of the world writes dates, with a letter stuck on the end.
dec.korean-batch-code-format.s0.b2: Many Korean packs also print the date in plain text next to the 제조 marking, or print a 까지 ("until") date, which is an expiry rather than a manufacture date. If both appear, the 까지 date is the one to trust.
dec.korean-batch-code-format.s1.b0: - Eight leading digits: read them as year, month, day — 20260118 is 18 January 2026.
dec.korean-batch-code-format.s1.b1: - Six leading digits: two-digit year, month, day — 231122 is 22 November 2023.
dec.korean-batch-code-format.s1.b2: - Any trailing letters are the production line. Ignore them.
dec.korean-batch-code-format.s2.b0: On the bottom of the tube or jar, on the crimp, or on the back of the carton — often alongside the 제조 label. Korean packaging is generally more forthcoming about dates than Western packaging, which is one reason K-beauty is easy to buy safely second-hand.
dec.korean-batch-code-format.faq0.a: "Manufactured". The date next to it is the production date. 까지 means "until" and marks an expiry date instead.
dec.korean-batch-code-format.faq1.a: COSRX, Laneige, Innisfree, Beauty of Joseon, Anua, Round Lab, SKIN1004, Missha, Etude, Sulwhasoo, Hera, Dr. Ceuracle and the rest of the Amorepacific and LG H&H stables, among others.
dec.korean-batch-code-format.faq2.q: My code decodes to a date a few months in the future.
dec.korean-batch-code-format.faq2.a: The decoder rejects future dates. If you are reading by hand and land in the future, you have probably read a day-month pair the wrong way round.
dec.beiersdorf-batch-code-format.title: NIVEA, Eucerin & Labello Batch Codes: Find & Check Yours
dec.beiersdorf-batch-code-format.desc: Check the batch code on NIVEA, Eucerin and Labello — enter it and get the production date and estimated shelf life in seconds, free.
dec.beiersdorf-batch-code-format.anat0: Last digit of the production year — 2018
dec.beiersdorf-batch-code-format.anat1: Week of that year — week 15 falls in April
dec.beiersdorf-batch-code-format.anat2: Internal batch reference — no date information
dec.beiersdorf-batch-code-format.ex0: Week 15 of a year ending in 8.
dec.beiersdorf-batch-code-format.ex1: The full 8-digit form, sometimes followed by two plant letters.
dec.beiersdorf-batch-code-format.s0.b0: Beiersdorf — NIVEA, Eucerin, Labello — is unusual in encoding the production week. The first digit is the last digit of the year; the next two are the week number, from 01 to 53.
dec.beiersdorf-batch-code-format.s0.b1: That gives you the date to within seven days, which for a body lotion or lip balm is precision to spare. The remaining digits are an internal batch reference.
dec.beiersdorf-batch-code-format.s1.b0: - First digit: last digit of the year.
dec.beiersdorf-batch-code-format.s1.b1: - Next two digits: the week number. Multiply by 7 to get a rough day of the year — week 15 is around day 105, so early April.
dec.beiersdorf-batch-code-format.s1.b2: - Ignore the rest of the code and any trailing letters.
dec.beiersdorf-batch-code-format.s2.b0: On the crimp of a tube (Labello, NIVEA hand cream), on the base of a tin (the classic NIVEA Creme tin has it stamped in the metal), or on the back of the carton. Eucerin, sold through pharmacies, usually carries an explicit expiry date as well — prefer that.
dec.beiersdorf-batch-code-format.faq0.a: Valid. Some years have 53 ISO weeks. A code claiming week 54 or higher is not a Beiersdorf week code.
dec.beiersdorf-batch-code-format.faq1.q: The NIVEA tin has no printed code.
dec.beiersdorf-batch-code-format.faq1.a: It is stamped into the metal of the base rather than printed. Tilt it against a light — embossed codes are almost invisible under direct light.
dec.bioderma-batch-code-format.title: Bioderma Batch Codes: Find & Check Yours
dec.bioderma-batch-code-format.desc: Check the batch code on Bioderma — enter it and get the production date and estimated shelf life free. Most Bioderma packs also print an expiry date.
dec.bioderma-batch-code-format.anat0: Day of the year — day 296 is 23 October
dec.bioderma-batch-code-format.anat1: Last digit of the production year — 2018
dec.bioderma-batch-code-format.s0.b0: Bioderma, owned by NAOS, writes the day of the year first — three digits, 001 to 366 — followed by the last digit of the production year. It is the exact reverse of Coty's YDDD, and reading one with the other's rule gives a wrong answer that looks right.
dec.bioderma-batch-code-format.s0.b1: Fortunately Bioderma makes the point moot on most products by printing an explicit expiry date on the pack. When a real expiry date exists, no decoding is needed and none should be trusted over it.
dec.bioderma-batch-code-format.s1.b0: - First three digits: the day of the year. Divide by about 30.4 for the month.
dec.bioderma-batch-code-format.s1.b1: - Fourth digit: the last digit of the year.
dec.bioderma-batch-code-format.s1.b2: - Then check the pack for a printed expiry date, which supersedes all of the above.
dec.bioderma-batch-code-format.faq0.a: It has preservatives and a long shelf life, but it is water-based, and water-based products are the ones that actually spoil. Once opened, follow the PAO symbol.
dec.the-ordinary-batch-code-format.title: The Ordinary Batch Codes: Find & Check Yours
dec.the-ordinary-batch-code-format.desc: Check the batch code on The Ordinary and other Deciem lines — enter it and get the production date and estimated shelf life free where the code supports it.
dec.the-ordinary-batch-code-format.anat0: Last digit of the production year — 2024
dec.the-ordinary-batch-code-format.anat1: Month letter: A = January … L = December
dec.the-ordinary-batch-code-format.s0.b0: Deciem's newer batch codes start with a year digit, then a month letter running A for January through L for December, then a line number. 4A01 is January 2024.
dec.the-ordinary-batch-code-format.s0.b1: The older four-character codes — TGD1, 1dg1 and similar — use an internal system Deciem has never published. They are not decodable, by us or by anyone else, and any site claiming to decode them is inventing the answer. On those products, use the printed best-before date.
dec.the-ordinary-batch-code-format.s1.b0: - First character a digit and second a letter A–L? Newer format. The digit is the year, the letter is the month.
dec.the-ordinary-batch-code-format.s1.b1: - Anything else — letters first, mixed case, no leading digit — is the old format. Don't try to decode it.
dec.the-ordinary-batch-code-format.s2.h: Why we stop at the month
dec.the-ordinary-batch-code-format.s2.b0: The trailing digits are ambiguous: some sources read them as a day of the month, others as a production line. We decode to the month rather than pick one and present a guessed day as fact. An unopened Ordinary serum has roughly a three-year shelf life, so a fortnight of imprecision changes nothing that matters.
Does vitamin C from The Ordinary really go off faster?
Pure L-ascorbic acid oxidises — that is what the yellow-to-brown colour shift tells you. Once it has turned, potency is gone regardless of what the batch code says.
dec.shiseido-batch-code-format.title: Shiseido Batch Codes: Find & Check Yours
dec.shiseido-batch-code-format.desc: Check the batch code on Shiseido and its houses — enter it and get the production date and estimated shelf life in seconds, free.
dec.shiseido-batch-code-format.anat0: Last digit of the production year — 2025
dec.shiseido-batch-code-format.anat1: Day of that year — day 29 is 29 January
dec.shiseido-batch-code-format.anat2: Plant / line letters — no date information
dec.shiseido-batch-code-format.ex1: The bare 4-digit form: day 233 of a year ending in 8.
dec.shiseido-batch-code-format.s0.b0: Shiseido uses the year-digit-then-Julian-day pattern, optionally followed by letters identifying the plant and line. The date is precise to the day.
dec.shiseido-batch-code-format.s0.b1: Japanese cosmetics regulation requires a printed expiry date on any product whose unopened shelf life is under three years. In practice that means most Shiseido skincare carries a date on the pack — the batch code is a cross-check, not the only source.
dec.shiseido-batch-code-format.s1.b0: - First digit: last digit of the year.
dec.shiseido-batch-code-format.s1.b1: - Next three: day of the year. Divide by about 30.4 for the month.
dec.shiseido-batch-code-format.s1.b2: - Trailing letters: plant and line. Ignore.
dec.shiseido-batch-code-format.faq0.a: Shiseido, Clé de Peau Beauté, NARS, IPSA, Elixir, Anessa and the group's other houses.
dec.rohto-batch-code-format.title: Rohto Batch Codes: Find & Check Yours
dec.rohto-batch-code-format.desc: Check the batch code on Hada Labo, Melano CC, OXY and Sunplay (Rohto) — get an estimated production date and shelf life free; the format is unofficial, so we flag the estimate.
dec.rohto-batch-code-format.anat0: Last digit of the production year — 2026
dec.rohto-batch-code-format.anat1: Month letter: A = January … L = December, so C = March
dec.rohto-batch-code-format.s0.h: An observed pattern, not a published one
dec.rohto-batch-code-format.s0.b0: Rohto has never published a batch-code scheme. What exists is a pattern documented by the community across many products: a year digit, a month letter running A for January through L for December, then a line digit.
dec.rohto-batch-code-format.s0.b1: We decode it, and we label the result low-confidence — because it is a reading of a pattern rather than an application of a documented rule. That distinction matters more than a confident answer does.
dec.rohto-batch-code-format.s1.b0: - First character: the year's last digit.
dec.rohto-batch-code-format.s1.b1: - Second character: the month letter. A is January and L is December — all twelve letters are used, with no skips.
dec.rohto-batch-code-format.s1.b2: - The result is a month, not a day.
dec.rohto-batch-code-format.faq0.a: Treat it as approximate. If the product carries a printed expiry date — many Japanese products do — use that instead.
dec.julian-date-codes.title: Batch Date Codes: Find & Check Yours
dec.julian-date-codes.desc: Check batch codes that print the date directly — enter the code from your product and get the production date and estimated shelf life in seconds, free.
dec.julian-date-codes.anat1: Day of that year — day 45 is 14 February
dec.julian-date-codes.ex0: Two year digits, then the day — the most common form.
dec.julian-date-codes.s0.b0: A Julian date, in manufacturing, is just the day's number in the year: 001 for 1 January, 032 for 1 February, 365 for 31 December in a common year. It exists because a three-digit day number is compact, unambiguous across languages, and trivially machine-readable — the same reasons food, pharmaceutical and automotive plants use it.
dec.julian-date-codes.s0.b1: Printed with a year, it fully specifies a date. The variations are only in the order and in how many year digits get printed: YYDDD, DDDYY, YYMMDD, DDMMYY.
dec.julian-date-codes.s1.h: Converting a day number in your head
dec.julian-date-codes.s1.b0: - Divide by 30.4 and round up. Day 045 ÷ 30.4 ≈ 1.5, so the 2nd month, February.
dec.julian-date-codes.s1.b1: - Month starts, roughly: Jan 001, Feb 032, Mar 060, Apr 091, May 121, Jun 152, Jul 182, Aug 213, Sep 244, Oct 274, Nov 305, Dec 335. Add one to each from March onwards in a leap year.
dec.julian-date-codes.s2.b0: This is the fallback. When a brand has no manufacturer-specific decoder — or when its own decoder fails on a code — the engine tries to read a date embedded in the digits and reports what it found, along with the interpretation it used.
dec.julian-date-codes.s2.b1: If more than one interpretation is possible, it says so. A code like 120312 is a valid date read three different ways, and no amount of cleverness resolves it without knowing who made the product. That is a limitation we surface rather than hide.
dec.julian-date-codes.faq0.q: Why doesn't every brand just print the date in plain English?
dec.julian-date-codes.faq0.a: Some do, and it is the honest thing to do. Batch codes exist for recalls and quality control, not for consumers — the fact they encode a date at all is incidental. Korean regulation forces the issue; most others don't.
dec.julian-date-codes.faq1.q: My code doesn't decode at all.
dec.julian-date-codes.faq1.a: Then it probably has no date in it. Some manufacturers use a pure sequence number. Check the pack for a printed expiry date, and use the PAO symbol for the period after opening.
